packs.name: code-review
packs.problem_class: Review code changes, pull requests, or existing code for security vulnerabilities, error handling gaps, code quality issues, and testing coverage
packs.mental_model: Code review is a security gate first, quality gate second. Every change is a potential attack surface, error source, or maintenance burden. Review in priority order: security, error handling, correctn
packs.evidence_summary: Retrofitted from Hermes code-review skill. Original skill is a checklist-based approach used by Hermes agents for PR rev

failure_cases: Security audit phase is language-generic — misses language-specific vulnerabilities (e.g., Go goroutine leaks, Rust unsafe blocks)
Code quality thresholds (50-line functions) are arbitrary and can be wrong for certain patterns (state machines, parsers)
Doesn't address performance review — a hot-path change could pass all criteria but introduce latency
Testing assessment assumes unit test paradigm — less applicable to integration-heavy or E2E-tested projects
Tone guidelines are hard to enforce programmatically — agent may still be overly critical

packs.name: plan
packs.problem_class: Create a concrete, actionable implementation plan for a software task without executing any changes
packs.mental_model: Planning is thinking made visible. A good plan separates understanding from doing — inspect the full context first, then write down exactly what to change, in what order, and how to verify each step.
packs.evidence_summary: Retrofitted from Hermes plan skill v1.0.0. The original skill defines plan-mode behavior for Hermes agents — read-only i

failure_cases: For trivial tasks (rename a variable, fix a typo), the 4-phase process is overkill — generates a 2-page plan for a 1-line change
Context inspection phase can be very slow on large monorepos — agent may read too many files
Plan quality depends heavily on the agent's ability to predict what will go wrong — junior agents produce optimistic plans
Assumes .hermes/plans/ directory convention — may not apply to all project structures
Plans can become stale quickly if the codebase changes between planning and execution

packs.name: systematic-debugging
packs.problem_class: Bug fixing, test failures, unexpected behavior — any technical issue requiring root cause analysis before fix
packs.mental_model: NEVER fix without understanding. 4 phases: Investigate → Pattern → Hypothesis → Fix. Each phase has a gate — you cannot proceed until the gate passes.
packs.evidence_summary: Reduced average debugging iterations from 5+ to 2-3 across multiple sessions. Prevents the #1 agent anti-pattern: applyi

failure_cases: Heisenbugs where debugging changes behavior (timing/race conditions)
Issues in third-party closed-source code where source inspection is impossible
When user explicitly wants a quick hotfix, not a root cause analysis

packs.name: quick-debug
packs.mental_model: Reproduce first, then isolate, then fix. Never guess at a fix without confirming you can see the bug happen. The fastest path to a fix is the shortest path through reproduce → isolate → fix → verify.
packs.evidence_summary: Used on 8 debugging tasks. 7 resolved on first pass, 1 required escalation (architectural issue). Average time to fix: 1

failure_cases: Does not help with intermittent timing/concurrency bugs — reproduce phase may not catch them
Overkill for trivial typos or syntax errors — just fix those directly
Isolation phase assumes access to logs/debugger — breaks in black-box scenarios

packs.name: test-driven-development
packs.problem_class: Implementing features or fixing bugs using a test-driven development workflow — writing tests first to define correctness, then implementing the minimal code to pass
packs.mental_model: TDD is a thinking tool, not just a testing technique. Writing the test first forces you to define what "correct" means before you write a line of implementation. The cycle is strict: Red (write a fail
packs.evidence_summary: Applied across 15 implementation tasks ranging from utility functions to API endpoints. Tasks using this TDD workflow ha

failure_cases: UI/visual code is hard to TDD — behavior tests work but pixel-perfect layout testing requires different tools
Exploratory/prototype work conflicts with TDD discipline — when you don't know what you're building, strict TDD adds friction
Legacy code without test infrastructure requires significant setup before the first test can even run
Performance-sensitive code may need optimization that tests don't drive — supplement with benchmarks
The minimal implementation phase can feel artificially slow — trust the process, it prevents over-engineering
